title: Cyclone Narelle pushes housing shortages deeper into crisis in Exmouth
full_text:
Tahila Paul was just sitting down with a coffee on the couch with her husband when Cyclone Narelle ripped the roof off her home.
"We were here while the roof peeled off, and within seconds of the roof coming off, the water just started pouring through the downlights and the fans," she said.
"Minutes after that, the ceiling gyprock just started falling down.
"There was nothing you could do."
Cyclone Narelle impacted three states, and as it passed Exmouth in Western Australia, it was a category three in strength.
Tahila and her husband had purchased their home less than a year ago, and had all but finished renovating a week before the cyclone.
Weeks after Narelle, she stands in the gutted wreckage, with no certainty of when she will be able to return.
"We're really lucky we've been able to stay at a friend's house for the next few weeks, but after that, to be honest, we haven't even really thought about it," she said.
"We're not sure — we have options to live with friends, so we are really lucky in that sense.
"I think it's going to come down to buying a caravan and living out the front in it.
"Because accommodation is tight in town and now trades are going to have to come into town, the town still needs tourism to come in."
Crisis point
While one-off disaster relief payments and energy bill relief from state and federal governments eased some of the financial burden, they did little to ease accommodation shortages.
Since the cyclone, Exmouth has struggled to welcome tourists with many expressing their frustrations.
However, out of more than 3,000 people who call Exmouth home, Ms Paul and her husband are not the only displaced locals struggling to find somewhere to stay.
Shire president Matt Niikkula said the housing crisis was something the shire was trying to address.
"The lack of housing is affecting so many people and as insurance is moving through and displacing more and more locals … we're scrambling to try to find accommodation for teachers, essential workers," Mr Niikkula said.
"Housing is our biggest crisis point at the moment.
"Trying to get enough trades into town to be able to fix these houses, enough insurance assessors … the accommodation lacks spread right across, and it's something that we're trying as a council, as the shire, very hard to try to get a hold of.
"From a local government perspective, we're trying to take into account everyone's needs and wants, and it's not all just about the loudest screamers."
No homes to live in
Ray White Exmouth principal licensee Mark Lucas put a call out for homes on social media after clients and families he worked with were displaced.
He expected the crisis to get worse.
"We've probably seen that number doubled now to probably closer to 10 to 15 per cent of our portfolios that are unusable properties and people being displaced and looking for somewhere else to live," Mr Lucas said.
He said that number could keep increasing.
"It's still evolving, so we're still having insurance assessors come to town and saying that properties are uninhabitable — that's a daily occurrence now.
"Caravan parks are closed, national parks closed — there is nowhere for people to go."
While Cyclone Narelle has further stressed the housing market, Mr Lucas said the problem stemmed from a lack of land releases, with the latest development — Sundrift — the first release in close to 20 years.
Mr Lucas said the new estate would provide some relief, but accommodation was still around 18 months away after titles were issued and houses built.
"I think what needs to happen, though, is that they need to be ready with the next [land release] after that," he said.
"You don't want to be going back to the very start of the investigation stage."
In a special council meeting in the weeks after Cyclone Narelle, the shire approved an overflow caravan park for self-contained caravans.
Mr Niikkula said the town had been amazing as it faced each challenge as part of the recovery from Cyclone Narelle.
"I think for me, the biggest takeaway is just the pride that I have in the town, in the community, of how we've all sort of come together and got through this as one," he said.
Tahila Paul agreed.
"The town's been amazing."
Australian Broadcasting Corporation
Document ABCNEW0020260506em56001mh
summary: Cyclone Narelle has exacerbated the housing crisis in Exmouth, Western Australia, displacing many residents and straining accommodation resources. Local officials are struggling to provide housing for displaced locals and essential workers, with a significant portion of properties deemed uninhabitable. The situation highlights the ongoing challenges in housing availability and the need for timely land releases to address future demands.

heading: General Insurance
key_points: Cyclone Narelle caused significant damage, displacing many residents in Exmouth.
The local housing crisis has worsened, with many properties uninhabitable and limited accommodation options.
Local government is working to address housing shortages and future land releases are critical.
relevance: This situation underscores the importance of risk assessment and financial planning in disaster recovery for actuaries and financial services professionals.
